data.title: Jeffries demands ban on deportation of US citizens as part of DHS reforms
data.description:
House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) on Thursday said House Democrats will demand a ban on the deportation of American citizens as a condition of winning their support for legislation funding the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).

“In what country are we living in if it’s controversial to prohibit [the deportation] of American citizens?” he said during a press briefing in the Capitol. “That shouldn’t even be a discussion.”

The DHS funding bill had already passed the House this month and was on its way to Senate approval, but it’s hit a wall in the wake of last Saturday’s fatal shooting of Alex Pretti, an ICU nurse, by federal immigration agents in Minneapolis. 

The killing has infuriated Democrats, who voted Thursday in the Senate to block a six-bill appropriations “minibus” to protest the inclusion of the DHS portion. The critics are vowing to continue that opposition until Republicans agree to new language in the DHS bill establishing guardrails on the conduct of the federal officers under the department. 

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer (D-N.Y.) on Wednesday laid out the changes he and Senate Democrats are demanding. They include new warrant requirements prior to searches and arrests; the establishment of a blanket code of conduct applying to all federal law enforcement personnel; a ban on agents wearing face masks; and new body camera mandates. 

“This is not border security, this is not law and order, this is chaos — created at the top and felt in so many of our neighborhoods,” Schumer said in announcing the list.

Jeffries broadly endorsed each of those reforms, though he cautioned that House Democrats will evaluate any legislative changes that come over from the Senate before endorsing any reform bills.

Jeffries’s proposed ban on the deportation of U.S. citizens goes a step further than Schumer did. He said it’s necessary given numerous reports indicating that American citizens have been wrongly deported as part of Trump’s aggressive deportation operations. DHS officials have denied that the agency is targeting or has deported U.S. citizens.

“In what country are we living in where ICE and DHS have free rein to detain and deport American citizens? That’s inconsistent with the Constitution,” he said. “And so if the extremists don’t get it, it should be made explicit.”

Jeffries also stipulated that there must be language in any new DHS bill empowering state and local law enforcers to conduct their own investigations, and perhaps bring their own charges in cases of alleged misconduct by DHS officers. 

“Those investigations cannot be conducted independently by the Department of Justice or by anyone closely associated with the Trump administration. They have no credibility to investigate anything,” Jeffries said. “And so, state and local law enforcement should explicitly have the authority to investigate crimes that are committed by DHS agents in their state and local jurisdictions.”

data.title: Watch live: Trump to unveil federal initiative addressing drug addiction
data.description:
President Trump on Thursday will sign an executive order that aims to coordinate a federal plan to combat drug addiction and substance abuse.

The “White House Great American Recovery Initiative” will advise federal agencies to issue grants in support of addiction recovery and education, according to the fact sheet obtained by CBS News. The panel will advise the federal government on how to integrate programs to aid with drug abuse prevention, intervention, treatment, recovery and support.

The order comes as the administration has ramped up its fight against drug trafficking, including deadly strikes on alleged narcotic-smuggling boats in the Caribbean.

The announcement from the Oval Office is scheduled to begin at 4 p.m. EST.

Watch the live video above.

data.title: Senate panel advances key crypto bill without Democratic support
data.description:
The Senate Agriculture Committee advanced part of a key cryptocurrency bill Thursday without Democratic support.

The panel voted 12-11 along party lines to advance its portion of the market structure legislation, which aims to clearly divide oversight of crypto markets between two financial regulators.

The vote marks a major step forward for the bill in the Senate, where Republicans and crypto-friendly Democrats have been negotiating for more than six months. But the lack of bipartisan support underscores the long and likely bumpy ahead for the legislation.

“While the conversations were cordial and substantive, what we found is that in some issues, there were fundamental policy disagreements,” Senate Agriculture Chair John Boozman (R-Ark.) said at Thursday’s markup.

“In a democracy, that’s okay,” he continued. “After months of work, we have made significant progress, really significant progress, working together. Now it’s time to move this process forward.”

Boozman and Sen. Cory Booker (D-N.J.), the top Democrat on the subcommittee that oversees digital assets, initially released a bipartisan market structure draft in November, although the text left several key issues unresolved.

This distinguished the panel from the Senate Banking Committee, where Republicans released a draft last July and have yet to secure support from crypto-friendly Democrats.

The Senate Agriculture Committee shares jurisdiction over the crypto bill with the Senate Banking Committee, as they respectively oversee the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) and Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) — the two regulators at issue in the bill.

Despite early bipartisan agreement, Boozman released a new draft ahead of Thursday’s markup that lacked Democratic support.

“I was excited,” Booker said Thursday, adding, “But then I come back to Washington and start the year with the news that my Republican colleagues were walking away from the bipartisan process.”

“To be clear, the product before us today is not the bipartisan draft that we were working on,” he continued. “It was negotiated with two parties at the table, but it isn’t in accord with the principles we laid out in November in a bipartisan way. However, despite this, I still deeply believe in the work that we’ve done.”

Democrats have continued to voice concerns with how the legislation handles ethics and national security issues, as well as the makeup of the CFTC.

Ethics has been a core sticking point, particularly as President Trump and his family have increasingly gotten involved in the crypto business, with ventures like World Liberty Financial and the $TRUMP memecoin.

“This is ridiculous that the president of the United States and his family have made billions of dollars off of this industry and are still trying to create a framework here without the kind of ethics that would prevent this kind of gross corruption in our country that would undermine our nation’s democracy,” Booker said.

Democrats have pushed for language in the market structure bill that would bar elected officials and their families from issuing or endorsing digital assets. Sen. Michael Bennet (D-Colo.) put forward an amendment Thursday proposing such a provision.

The amendment was ultimately voted down, with Boozman suggesting the issue falls outside the jurisdiction of the Senate Agriculture Committee.

The makeup of the CFTC has also been a point of concern. The agency, which is typically headed up by a five-person bipartisan commission, currently has only one commissioner — CFTC chair Michael Selig. Selig was nominated by Trump and confirmed by the Senate last month.

This comes as the relatively low-profile agency is poised to take on significant new responsibilities overseeing crypto markets. Democrats have argued for a provision requiring the CFTC have a minimum number of members from both parties to move forward with rulemaking.

Despite the partisan nature of Thursday’s markup, several Democrats underscored that they are hoping to continue bipartisan negotiations.

“I expect the vote today in this committee will probably fall on party lines,” Sen. Adam Schiff (D-Calif.) said. “That is not reflective, though, I think of the progress that we’ve made.”

Boozman told reporters after the vote that senators would be continuing talks Thursday afternoon.

“I think we reached a little bit of an impasse, and so we felt like we needed to move it forward, and this just gives us the opportunity to have something that has passed and be able to change it as we need to,” he added.

The Senate Agriculture Committee vote comes after a markup in the Senate Banking Committee was postponed earlier this month in the face of industry concerns.

Senate Banking Chair Tim Scott (R-S.C.) made the announcement the night before the markup was set to go forward, after Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong said he would not be able to support the latest version of the bill.

The panel had released a new draft of its portion of the legislation ahead of the markup. A provision on stablecoin yield, in particular, faced pushback from the crypto industry, which has lobbied against such language for months.

data.title: Schumer lays out immigration demands to avoid government shutdown
data.description:
Democrats have been getting hammered with the same question for months: Where’s your plan?  

Well, now they’re putting it on paper. And it’s really not some abstract, academic wish list; it’s three very specific demands tied to Homeland Security funding, coming as the clock ticks toward a shutdown deadline and after federal agents shot and killed Alex Pretti in Minneapolis last weekend.  

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer says his caucus is united around three main objectives, and when you listen to what they are, the bigger point is simple: immigration enforcement can exist, but it can’t operate with no rules, no transparency and no accountability.  

  
“First we need to end roving patrols,” the New York Democrat said. “We want to tighten the rules governing the use of warrants and require ICE coordination with state and local law enforcement.” 

“Second, we want to enforce accountability; there needs to be a uniform code of conduct and accountability. Federal agents should be held to the same use of force policies that apply to state and local law enforcement and be held accountable when they violate these rules. We need independent investigations to help enforce accountability.”  

That’s common sense. Because “roving patrols” without clear rules don’t just create fear, they create mistakes. They create confrontations. And they create the kind of murky, confusing encounters where nobody knows who’s in charge, what authority is being used, or what rights people actually have in that moment.  

And that’s where the second point matters: a universal code of conduct and independent investigations. If local police departments can be required to follow use-of-force policies, federal agents shouldn’t get a special carve-out. Accountability isn’t anti-law enforcement — it’s what makes law enforcement legitimate.  

Then there’s the third demand, and honestly, this one shouldn’t even be controversial:  

“Third, we want masks off, body cameras on. Federal agents must be prohibited from wearing masks, they must be required to wear body cameras and they also must always have to carry proper identification.”  

Masks off. Body cameras on. Proper identification. That’s not about politics, it’s about basic transparency.  

Body cameras have been used across the country because they protect the public and they protect officers when accusations fly. And if someone is exercising government power — stopping you, detaining you, entering your home — you should be able to see their face and know who they are.  

The public is clearly uneasy with how far things are going right now. A Reuters/Ipsos poll found that some 58 percent of respondents said ICE agents have gone “too far” in their crackdown. That’s not a fringe opinion, that’s a majority.  

So when people say Democrats don’t have a plan, here it is: guardrails, accountability, and transparency. Because enforcing immigration law should never mean abandoning the standards that make this country a democracy in the first place. 

Lindsey Granger is a NewsNation contributor and co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising.” This column is an edited transcription of her on-air commentary.

data.title: Why 7 GOP senators voted against bill to keep government open past Friday
data.description:
Seven Republican senators on Thursday voted against advancing a six-bill funding package to keep the government open past Friday, citing concerns about the ballooning federal deficit and what they saw as the failure of the so-called appropriations minibus to significantly cut spending

Sens. Tedd Budd (R-N.C.), Ron Johnson (R-Wis.), Mike Lee (R-Utah), Ashley Moody (R-Fla.), Rand Paul (R-Ky.), Rick Scott (R-Fla.) and Tommy Tuberville (R-Ala.) voted against a motion to advance the bill, even though it had the strong support of Senate GOP leaders.

The package, which was blocked on a 45-55 vote, would have failed regardless of how the Republicans voted. It needed 60 votes to advance and did not pick up any Democratic support.

Paul said his opposition to the package was driven by “a general complaint about how much government spends.”

“Most of the spending levels have come back either at last year’s level or above. Last year we had a deficit of about $1.8 [trillion], $1.9 trillion. If you vote for these levels, it’s sort of a belief that you think what we’re spending is ok,” he said.

“I think what we’re spending is too much,” he added.

Scott said he opposes Democratic demands to pull funding for the Department of Homeland Security out of the funding package after Democrats negotiated a variety of spending victories for their own side.

He also pointed to what he called wasteful spending in the package.

“I don’t like the bill. I don’t like the six-bill package because it doesn’t balance the budget and it’s got all this wasteful spending through all these earmarks,” he said.

“Now, they’re talking about proceeding to cut out the ICE portion of the funding. I don’t support that, I support ICE. I think it’s crazy that at the last minute Democrats think they can dictate and shutd own the [Homeland Security] funding,” Scott said.

“They want all their wasteful spending, they don’t want to balance the budget. Then they don’t want to fund the thing they caused … they allowed all these criminals into our country and now ICE is doing their job and [Democrats] don’t want to fund it,” he said.

Sen. Ashley Moody (R-Fla.) declined to say why she voted against the package, telling The Hill she didn’t have time to talk as she walked into a lunch meeting.

Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) cast the eighth Republican vote against the bill, but he did so on purely procedural grounds. He cast a last-minute vote against the motion to end debate on proceeding to the bill so that he could enter a “motion to recommit” in order to have flexibility to bring it back to the floor quickly.

data.title: DOJ files charges against alleged Omar attacker
data.description:
The Department of Justice (DOJ) has filed charges against the man who attacked Rep. Ilhan Omar (D-Minn.) at a recent press conference, The Associated Press reported. 

Anthony Kazmierczak, a 55-year-old man, is charged with forcibly assaulting, opposing, impeding and intimidating Omar after he sprayed the Minnesota congresswoman with a mixture of water and apple cider vinegar at the Tuesday event. 

The man was arrested at the scene by law enforcement and charged with third-degree assault, the Minneapolis Police Department confirmed. 

According to the DOJ’s court affidavit, Kazmierczak yelled, “She’s not resigning. You’re splitting Minnesotans apart.” Authorities also said he previously told an associate that “somebody should kill” Omar.

The congresswoman, who has been an outspoken critic of the Trump administration’s crackdown on immigration in Minnesota, was calling for the resignation of Department of Homeland Security (DHS) Secretary Kristi Noem when she was attacked. 

DHS has led a massive immigration operation in the state, during which federal authorities have shot and killed two Minnesotans and shot a Venezuelan migrant in the leg while he attempted to evade arrest.

Following Tuesday’s attack, Omar continued with the town hall and her office reported in a statement posted on the social platform X on Tuesday evening that the congresswoman was “okay.” 

“She continued with her town hall because she doesn’t let bullies win,” the statement said. 

State Gov. Tim Walz (D) condemned the attack in a Tuesday post on the social platform X. 

“Our state has been shattered by political violence in the last year,” Walz wrote. “The cruel, inflammatory, dehumanizing rhetoric by our nation’s leaders needs to stop immediately.”

A day after the town hall, President Trump accused Omar of orchestrating the attack, telling ABC News, “She probably had herself sprayed, knowing her.” However, Trump acknowledged that he hadn’t seen the video clip of the attack. 

Thursday’s update from the DOJ follows the FBI’s announcement a day prior that it would be taking charge “to maintain the integrity of the investigation.”

data.title: Trump officials met with Canadian separatists; British Columbia premier alleges 'treason'
data.description:
The Trump administration has reportedly taken meetings with a fringe, far-right separatist group from the oil-rich province of Alberta, Canada, amid rock-bottom relations between Washington and Ottawa.  

British Columbia Premier David Eby on Thursday sharply criticized the meetings between the Alberta Prosperity Project (APP) and the Trump administration as amounting to “treason,” responding to reporting by the Financial Times. 

“To go to a foreign country and to ask for assistance in breaking up Canada, there’s an old-fashioned word for that. And that word is treason,” Eby told reporters in Ottawa, when told of the Times report. 

The White House did not deny the meetings but said in a statement to The Hill that “administration officials meet with a number of civil society groups. No support or commitments were conveyed.”

The State Department and the APP did not immediately return a request for comment. 

The reported meetings come amid a widening rift between the U.S. and Canada, with President Trump repeatedly disparaging America’s northern neighbor and its independence, saying it should become the 51st U.S. state. Trump has also imposed tariffs on Canada and threatened more. 

Trump took a swipe at Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney just last week after the Canadian leader delivered a widely regarded speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, on the “rupture” of the U.S.-led world order.

“Canada lives because of the United States. Remember that, Mark, the next time you make your statements,” Trump said during his Davos speech. 

U.S. officials met three separate times with members of the APP since April of last year, the Financial Times reported. The outlet also said the APP is seeking another meeting next month with State and Treasury Department officials to ask for a $500 billion credit facility to help fund the province in the event of a successful independence referendum.

“The U.S. is extremely enthusiastic about a free and independent Alberta,” Jeff Rath, a legal counsel for the APP who attended the meetings, told the FT.

Last week, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said he viewed Alberta as “a natural partner for the U.S.” and “I think we should let them come down into the U.S…. They have great resources. The Albertans are very independent people.”

Bessent made the comments in an interview with conservative podcast host Jack Posobiec.

Bessent continued that there’s a “rumor” an independence referendum will happen later this year and said the Canadian government is blocking the construction of a pipeline in the province.

“Alberta has a wealth of natural resources, but they won’t let them build a pipeline to the Pacific,” Bessent said.

The group Stay Free Alberta is reportedly gathering signatures for a referendum, with the group needing to collect more than 170,000 signatures by May to hold a vote. 

The FT reported a person familiar with Bessent’s thinking said neither he nor any other Treasury officials were aware of any credit facility proposal and did not intend to engage on the issue. 

A survey earlier this month found that only three in 10 people in Alberta and Quebec supported independence, and those numbers declined further when respondents were pressed with questions on the consequences of severing from Ottawa.

data.title: Four senators now running for governor: Who are they?
data.description:
Sen. Amy Klobuchar’s (D-Minn.) announcement Thursday that she’s running for Minnesota governor makes her the fourth lawmaker in the U.S. Senate to launch a gubernatorial campaign. 

Klobuchar joins a growing number of politicians looking to get out of Washington, D.C., and back to their home states.

Sens. Tommy Tuberville (R-Ala.), Marsha Blackburn (R-Tenn.) and Michael Bennet (D- Colo.) are also running for governor in their respective home states, which means this election cycle will have the most senators running for governor in history, according to an Associated Press analysis. 

Here’s a look at each senator’s gubernatorial campaign. 

Tuberville

Tuberville launched his Republican bid for governor of Alabama during a Fox News interview in May. 

The senator has gained the endorsement of President Trump, of whom Tuberville has been a strong ally during his time in Congress. Tuberville, a former head football coach at Auburn University, is considered one of the most conservative members of the Senate, according to independent congressional research service Gov Track. 

“As your next governor, I’ll fight for President Trump’s America First agenda, stop illegal immigration dead in its tracks and protect our conservative values,” Tuberville said in a campaign video posted on the social platform X. “After doing all that, the enemies of Alabama are going to call me a lot of things. With your help, governor will be one of them.”

In the Senate, Tuberville serves as the chair of the Senate Armed Services Committee’s Personnel subcommittee, as well as the Health, Education, Labor and Pensions’ American Family subcommittee. 

In his run for governor, Tuberville aims to counter “woke ideology,” promote job growth, improve education and support the state’s agricultural industry, according to his campaign website. 

The senator’s campaign is currently being challenged by his Republican opponent, who claims Tuberville does not meet the 7-year residency requirement to run for governor. Ken McFeeters filed a challenge against Tuberville on Tuesday, claiming that Tuberville primarily resides in Florida, CNN first reported. 

This is not the first time the former football coach has faced controversy during a campaign. He was accused of violating the STOCK Act after he did not meet a campaign finance deadline during his 2021 Senate race. 

Alabama’s 2026 primary is May 19.

Bennet

In a campaign announcement in April, Bennet said he is joining the governor’s race, running as a Democrat, to counter issues with housing and food costs, childcare and access to mental health services. 

“Our best solutions to these challenges will not come from the broken politics in Washington, it will come from us,” Bennet said. 

The senator is running in a crowded race to succeed Colorado’s term-limited governor, Jared Polis (D). Over 40 people from both parties are vying for the seat, including several state legislators and a convicted murderer. 

Bennet’s platform includes promises to bring down the costs of everyday goods, build affordable homes, cut greenhouse gas emissions and invest in public education for Coloradans. 

A vocal opponent of President Trump, Bennet accused the president of ‘taking a wrecking ball to our economy’ in his campaign announcement.

The legislator serves as the ranking member on a subcommittee for Conservation, Forestry, Natural Resources and Biotechnology, which is under the Senate’s Committee on Agriculture, Nutrition and Forestry. 

Colorado’s 2026 primary is June 30.

Klobuchar

The federal government’s massive immigration crackdown in the state and a widespread welfare fraud scandal have heightened political tensions in the North Star State. 

In a video announcing her campaign on Thursday, Klobuchar, running as a Democrat, addressed the situation in her home state, saying, “These times call for grit and resilience.”

“We cannot sugarcoat how hard this is, but in these moments of enormous difficulty, we find strength in our Minnesota values of hard work, freedom and simple decency and good will,” she said. “These times call for leaders who can stand up and not be rubber stamps of this administration, but who are also willing to find common ground and fix things in our state.”

Gov. Tim Walz (D) announced earlier this month that he would not seek reelection following intense pressure from the Trump administration, who said Walz failed to intervene in a fraud scheme that federal prosecutors say diverted billions of dollars allocated for state social services. 

In her announcement video, Klobuchar referenced her previous role as the Hennepin County, Minnesota, county attorney, where she handled criminal prosecution cases. 

The senator currently serves as the ranking member on the Senate’s Agriculture, Nutrition and Forestry Committee. In this role, the senator pushed back against the U.S. Department of Agriculture’s (USDA) policy at the end of last year that required all people who receive Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program (SNAP) reapply for these benefits. 

Klobuchar previously ran in the 2020 presidential election, dropping out a day before Super Tuesday to endorse former President Joe Biden (D). 

If elected, Klobuchar would be the first female governor of Minnesota. She expressed her hope to be a “transformative governor” for the state in her Thursday announcement.

“Now is our moment to renew our commitment to the common good,” she said. 

The 2026 Minnesota primary is Aug. 11.

Blackburn

Blackburn, running as a Republican, launched her gubernatorial campaign in Tennessee in August. The staunch Trump ally is running to replace term-limited Gov. Bill Lee (R). 

In a campaign video announcing her candidacy, the senator outlined her top campaign priorities as job creation, the deportation of illegal immigrants and the promotion of a pro-life and anti-trans agenda. 

“I believe in Tennesseans, and I’m ready to deliver the kind of conservative leadership that will ensure our state is America’s conservative leader for this generation and the next,” Blackburn said in the video. 

Gov Track identifies Blackburn as the second-most conservative serving in the Senate. The senator has been the primary sponsor on 17 bills that have been enacted, including a 2024 bill that strengthens the reporting process for incidents relating to the sexual exploitation of children.  

If elected, Blackburn would be the first female governor of Tennessee.

Tennessee’s 2026 primary is Aug. 6.

data.title: These companies are matching Trump Account contributions for employees
data.description:
A number of companies have pledged to match the federal government’s $1,000 deposits in the “Trump accounts” for the children of employees. 

The Treasury Department is contributing $1,000 to the accounts of children born between Jan. 1, 2025, through Dec. 31, 2028. Employers of the child or their parent can contribute up to $2,500 per year to the accounts starting on July 5, according to the IRS. 

So far this week, Coinbase, JPMorgan Chase, Steak ‘n Shake and Turning Point USA have announced they will match that $1,000 contribution for the children of their employees. 

“JPMorganChase has demonstrated a long-term commitment to the financial health and well-being of all of our employees and their families around the world, including more than 190,000 here in the United States,” JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon said in a release. “By matching this contribution, we’re making it easier for them to start saving early, invest wisely, and plan for their family’s financial future.”

Turning Point USA CEO Erika Kirk wrote on social platform X Wednesday that the organization and its affiliate, Turning Point Action, will match Treasury’s contribution dollar-for-dollar “for every eligible employee’s newborn baby.”

“We’re proud to stand with @POTUS in supporting families and investing in the future of America,” she added.

During a Wednesday event touting the accounts, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said that Broadcom, Intel, Comcast and Chipotle all pledged to match the $1,000 contribution earlier in the day. President Trump also said at the event that Continental Resources, Nvidia and Uber will also match the government’s deposit. 

The Hill has reached out to those companies to confirm their plans.

“Donations from employers are essential to bringing the President’s vision to life,” Bessent noted.

Other companies, such as BlackRock, BNY, Charles Schwab, Charter Communications, Robinhood and SoFi previously pledged to match the government’s contribution to the accounts of employee’s children. 

Employer deposits, which are pre-tax, count against the $5,000 annual cap on individual contributions to the accounts. Family members, friends and other adults can contribute starting in July. 

Charitable organizations and state government may also make contributions, which do not count against the annual limit. 

Last month, billionaire Michael Dell and his wife, Susan, pledged to donate $6.25 billion to the accounts of 25 million children, amounting to $250 per child. 

Dell said at Wednesday’s event that their donations are targeted to zip codes where the median income is less than $150,000.

Trump also praised singer Nicki Minaj, who attended the summit, for pledging to contribute “hundreds of the thousands of dollars” to the accounts of her fans’ children. The exact amount and qualification details are unclear.

data.title: Insane! CNN pundit still denies Charlie Kirk was killed by left-winger
data.description:
Why are left-wingers STILL denying that Charlie Kirk was killed by someone on their team? It’s been months, and I thought this vicious lie had been debunked numerous times. But here we go again. 

Tiffany Cross is a progressive pundit and podcast host. She previously had a show on MSNBC, and now makes occasional appearances on CNN. This week, she joined the panel for Abby Phillip’s show, alongside legal analyst Elie Honig, a moderate, and TV personality Kevin O’Leary, a libertarian-leaning conservative. It did not go well.  

Right off the bat, Cross repeated the provably false claim that Kirk was murdered by a right-wing extremist. And she conjured up this lie during a discussion of the recent attack on Rep. Ilhan Omar, who was assaulted by a deranged man who squirted an unknown liquid substance at her while she was speaking. Honig condemned this act of political violence — as everyone should — but then made the very fair observation that there is plenty of extremism on both sides. He went out of his way to assert that he did not necessarily think there were exactly equal amounts on both sides, only that conservative figures faced violence, too. But when he mentioned Kirk, Cross interrupted him to say that Kirk had been killed by a right-wing person.  

That is a lie.  

This notion that Tyler Robinson, Kirk’s alleged killer, had right-wing motivations has apparently remained appealing for leftists, ever since the progressive Substack writer Heather Cox Richardson helped to mainstream this idea in the immediate aftermath of the shocking murder. Nevertheless, it is absolutely false. While Robinson did indeed come from a Republican family, his own motivation for committing the assassination was that he disliked the things Kirk had said about transgender people. We know this because Robinson confessed it in messages to his romantic partner, a transgender individual, writing that he felt compelled to take action to stop Kirk from spreading hate against the transgender community. 

This is not really open to debate. Robinson is not a right-wing extremist, and his stated reasons for murdering Kirk were characteristic of left-wing thinking. 

Perhaps you can forgive a commentator for getting this wrong if it’s before all the facts come in, but that at this point — months after Kirk’s death — Cross is just repeating something that is completely debunked. 

She also argued with O’Leary about whether new recruits who have joined Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) are members of the Proud Boys, a far-right militia group. Cross asserted that she knew for a fact that Proud Boys had joined ICE, and cited as evidence the presence of what she described as “white supremacist” tattoos on the necks of some ICE officers. 

Now, there may be former Proud Boys who are members of law enforcement. And there might be members of law enforcement who have white nationalist tattoos. It’s true that one ICE agent attracted attention for having a Norse tattoo that some on the far-right claim as their own. I would caution about reading too much into this, however. Tattoos mean different things to different people. For 20 years, Graham Platner, a Democratic candidate for Senate in Maine, had a tattoo plausibly associated with Nazi iconography on his chest. He claims he didn’t know that that’s what a totenkopf is. 

But Cross suggested that there were so many Proud Boys joining ICE that it was having an effect on the former group’s organizing. “There’s a reason why we have not seen a resurgence of the Proud Boys,” she said, “and that is because I believe a lot of them are likely made ICE officers.” 

That is NOT the reason we’ve seen less from the Proud Boys. We’ve seen less from the Proud Boys because they were sued, and many of them were in jail following January 6th. Enrique Tarrio, former chairman of the Proud Boys, was imprisoned for two years after being convicted of having organized the riot at the U.S. Capitol. Tarrio was also revealed to have been an FBI informant in 2012 and 2014.  

So in that sense, yeah, there have definitely been Proud Boys working for the federal government. But somehow, I don’t think that’s what Cross meant. 

Robby Soave is co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising” and a senior editor for Reason Magazine. This column is an edited transcription of his daily commentary.
